item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, I'd love to know what exactly happens if SCP-1384 reaches the exit of the tunnel +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good love the idea that we don't even know all of the rules and are just kind of guessing +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's really interesting in retrospect how much this one parallels 4000 - the stealing of names, the confusing lists of rules, the general fae flavour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how I didn't find this one till now, but jesus christ, this is cool.

Fun, interesting, full of all kinds of hook, and room for more.

Emphatic +1 for this, and the supplement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The squares can be damaged, but return to an undamaged condition as soon as SCP-1384 moves to a new square.

If I take a sample of a square, and SCP-1384 moves and "heals" the square, do I still have my sample?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely brilliant, and I wish I'd discovered it sooner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of Tzeentch or one of his daemons. "Just as planned!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably one of my favorite SCPs on the site. The dialogue has that distinct Mann flavor that makes other Mann skips like SCP-1171 so enjoyable, the rules that the entity follows are all extremely intriguing and leave much to the imagination, and the general premise is a brilliant one that I'm surprised no one had used sooner- not just in SCP format, but in literature in general. Enthusiastic upvote.

+For those who don't get it
-Keep in mind this may not be correct

So if you play chess, you probably already know what a pawn is and how you promote it, and you should have an inkling of what's going on. But if you don't play chess, it's understandable if you're confused. Basically, a pawn is the simplest and most abundant game piece; you start the game with eight of them, while you only start with two copies of some other pieces and only one copy of the king and queen. Pawns also have the shortest range of movement of all the pieces; they can only move one tile forward, while the other pieces can move all around the board in different directions. Due to their general uselessness, pawns are typically used as sacrifices, in which the player purposely puts a piece in danger of being eaten in order to further their own position and strategical advantage. However, if you manage to somehow get a pawn all the way to the other side of the board (which would take tons of planning and foresight), you have the ability to "promote" the pawn, transforming it into a queen, the most powerful piece in the game.

The implication in this skip seems to be that this entity is a literal pawn in some extra-dimensional game of chess played by two unknown entities, and it is trying to get to the end of the tunnel so it can promote itself. What it turns into after it is promoted, we have no idea. What the rules are in this game of chess, we have no idea. But it's probably in our best interest that we put the entity responsible in checkmate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand anything about this. And it's frustrating because the article itself drew me in and the dialogue in it wasn't overly obnoxious. The Document did absolutely nothing to help me comprehend or get a better feel for anything about the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think he sent the D class back to the start of life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to know more about teh game! grah +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the hell is all of this it's so brilliant
seriously though this is so much what.
They should start attempting strategic moves to…
…opposing color? Why yellow of all things? Why yellow? Why not blue or black or white or…
New D-class squares being red… did he send that D-class to the start… of the game he's playing? So now the D-class is playing too?

white, yellow, black, blue and orange

Those are the colors. Researchers wear white. Agents wear… I don't know… Anglican vicars wear black (though they swapped to purple recently). D-classes wear orange. I don't know why the D-class's thing added red.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the amount of work done with this article, and sadly, it failed to draw me in or make me interested in any kind of mystery surrounding the SCP. Knowing my eventual cluelessness, the comments didn't hide any hints to help me understand what is so likable about this SCP either. Sorry.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: 22 hours. But I think 1171 came up while there were more eyes on the site, so it was timing more than anything else.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: 1981 hit 100 pretty dang fast. Dunno if it was less than 17 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… Oh, wow. That is clever. Man, I wish I'd done that intentionally. It's actually just a reference to Geoffrey Fisher, Archbishop of Canterbury.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or that Bobby Fischer was a famous chess player?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: About 17 hours, actually, so this one doesn't quite make the record. I'm still quite happy and gratified that this has been well received.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Nope. Mann's own SCP-1171 got there in like three days.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Holy shit. That's… just under a week? Is that the fastest +100 ever?
Very intriguing, Mann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After trying to understand this with my tiny mind, I give up.
It's probably just a big ol' game of Calvin Ball or something.

Also, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing it has to do with "Fishers of men" or the fact that Jesus is represented with a fish sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … oh my god it actually IS a pawn. Or perhaps a checker.

item.title: Very nice work.
item.content:encoded:
This is very, very clever. I'm enjoying this one immensely. It's the first entity that I could imagine actively engaging with that I've read in quite some time.

I've not really read your other SCP entries, I seem to recall mostly Tales. I really should rectify this promptly, if this is your style.

Wonderings:

What the personality is like of the opposing side…
What is the significance of the blue tiles…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, heck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The minute "this is an x that does y" was considered valid criticism, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, and then in rule #9 he says he'll "get to the side eventually".
So maybe escape is only secondary to the promotion he'd get from reaching the far edge of the board.

Edit: Sniped!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I assume right, the real issue would be what it is gonna become once it reaches the other side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might be even simpler than that, Wishun.

Consider its antipathy for things that are yellow. It doesn't acknowledge things that are yellow, but won't step on a yellow tile, because it's an opposing color. Might be that other colors affect it as well. The question therefore would be whether other colors affect it as well. (as an aside, I'm fighting spelling the word "colours", because I'm American, even though the SCP is in Scotland.)

Then you ask what colors agents tend to wear, what colors vicars (if Anglican, priests if not) wear, and what colors researchers tend to wear. (I talk with Dr. Mann in chat, so that one he hinted at)

Now, consider that the Taker of Turns talks in game metaphors, mentioned that he was put here by his own side (who "played the sacrifice"), and has sympathy toward pawns, both in chess and in perceptions.

What might be the Taker of Turns be, then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A massively powerful magical being, bound by its nature (or possibly mystic law) to follow arbitrary rules, but perfectly willing and able to bend those rules into a pretzel given the slightest opening…
I like the feel of this, very much in keeping with old world fae.

I'm putting myself in the Foundation's position, and it seems like they are overlooking some important angles. Some of which might greatly facilitate containment.

First, it thinks the priest had a better chance of keeping it bound than the Foundation does. They should really be trying to find out why that is. My first thought is to try some faith-based measures. Has anyone thought to ask if Agent Lachner is particularly religious?

Second, they may not be able to alter the squares, but why not paint a wide swath of the floor outside the "game board" yellow? That way, he can't leave even when he gets to the edge. It's at least worth trying.

Third, it likes chess, and is not supernaturally good at it. Combined with its views on following agreements to the exact letter, why not offer a wager? One game of chess, if the researcher wins, it doesn't move for 5 years. Or maybe gamble for it to honestly and fully explain the rules it moves under. If nothing else, what it asks for as a counter-stake would reveal something about its nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "It exists" is a theme now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have dreams about this sort of thing.

Kinda unconventionally written… but with unadulterated perspective.

I don't quite understand the significance with the tile colours, though.

The whole thing with the rules is quite interesting… there are bits that are gimmicky and bits that are good, but overall I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one. Pretty unique. Weird. And scary, too. We don't know what's going to happen when it gets out.

I'm not sure I quite get it, though. I know it's some form of chess piece. I don't get the significance of yellow, or the "I'm not a Pict" line. And what exactly happened to the D-Class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loving this, Found it fun to visualise in my head.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I should've guessed this was Mann's. There's a distinct flavour to your narrative and dialogue bits— and I mean that in a good way. The voices are almost audible.

"This includes seemingly innocuous or abstract things such as "the time," or one's name." is when I knew this was going to be interesting. It still amazes me how a little detail, just enough enigma, can take something from generic reality-bender to intriguing mystery. You could come up with a dozen endings to this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These puns are leaving me green around the gills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've no idea why, but in my head, I read the SCP's voice as sounding just like the main character in this youtube video. "There you are sir! On time as usual."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought "Eh, it's alright."

Then I read the attached doc. It really brought the SCP to life; have an upvote, you earned it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I'm not a Pict, you know." +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what's you're on about, Sorts, unless you're Anglican for something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He contacted his superiors in the church, who ultimately contacted the Foundation through the Fisher Protocols.

I see what you did therrrrrre

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this quite a bit, especially with the attached rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an awful lot going on here, which is one of the things that makes this so enjoyable. But best of all is we have no idea what will happen if it gets out. This isn't the blue man walking out of the temporal rupture at .6c.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this thing, perchance, related to SCP-1918 at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no guarantee that it will stop forever at a yellow square. It's obvious that this thing follows a lotof rules, most likely much more than the Foundation knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except if it moves towards the exit on its own when left alone it would get out unless there is a yellow square in its direct path and nothing happens to let it to move around it.

What makes you say it wouldn't?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a computer. But it can't do anything without information being given to it. Yes, it moves if you leave it alone…but that doesn't mean it'll be able to *get out* on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting setup but it feels cliched, like four different SCP themes, not ideas but themes, combined. It exists, it can't be destroyed, it wants to leave, it has rules. And-? No downvote, but I don't feel anything for this scip without some kind of personality to give it flavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I was eh. The tone seemed off, and the effects were just a little too odd for me. Yet after rereading, and reading the attached doc, it's grown on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand the appeal of this article, or what makes it interesting.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FRICK, I also just realized something.

Writing about it.

Writing about it, and documenting it, HAS to give it some advantadge.

That's one of the reasons it felt it would get out sooner if we kept trying to contain it.

That's why the Vicar, despite his apparent desire to keep the entity contained (and knowing what he did from the hint, his ability to DRIVE IT BACK a bit), didn't leave any notes. Why wouldn't he leave notes, if he was interested in keeping it stuck? At the very least, to aid whoever followed him? Because writing about it, documenting it, gives it some sort of advantage.

Frick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why can I not help but think of Good Omens?

"God does not play dice with the universe; He plays an ineffable game of His own devising, which might be compared, from the perspective of any of the other players [i.e. everybody], to being involved in an obscure and complex variant of poker in a pitch-dark room, with blank cards, for infinite stakes, with a Dealer who won't tell you the rules, and who smiles all the time.” "

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So delightfully weird!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Intriguing! Complicated and long, but keeps me interested and wanting more. This is a unique, new spin on the tired old wish granter. Obvious upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ranging in height from 1.5 to 2 meters in height.

Redundancy

SCP-1384 was found inside a tunnel two hundred feet

GRRRRRRRRRRRRR

Otherwise, I like it a lot. The supplementary document really makes it; I was kinda "meh" until I got to all the rules. Now I'm guessing about what would happen if he got out, who devised these rules, etc. And when an SCP leaves me thinking, I upvote it, so here ya go. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So you just mixed Chess, random actions, uknown rules and a (probably) bounded Djinn in a single article… Epic success! And seriously, stealing the researcher's name totally got me by surprise. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! A new SCP from Mann. Huzzah! Now this is some great tale material. +1
